coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the electrochemical threshold properties of novel aluminum‑copper bimetallic foils anodes for high reversibility li-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: de Prado, Javier
Børresen, Børre T.
Utrilla, Victoria
Ureña, Alejandro
coredata.dc:description: High-reversibility aluminum‑copper bimetallic anodes have been characterized to determine the threshold specific capacity necessary to ensure a highly reversible process when used as anodes in Li-ion batteries. By controlling the intercalation step, the formation of a β-AlLi surface layer on the aluminum side is promoted. This layer effectively mitigates the stress generated during the expansion and contraction associated to the charging/discharging process. The threshold specific capacity was assessed by applying four different charging steps, corresponding to 5 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 50 % of the experimental specific capacity of aluminum, over 100 galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles. Additionally, the effects of different current densities (0.5 and 1 mA/cm2) and two aluminum qualities (1XXX and 3XXX) on the reversibility of the reactions and capacity loss were analyzed. The results indicated that charging steps corresponding to a specific capacity of 200 mA.h.g−1 were identified as the threshold capacity for achieving reversible anode behavior. Although this property is the most critical factor, the threshold point also depends on various factors such as the aluminum alloy used for the bimetallic anodes and the current density applied. The use of the 3XXX alloy, which is underexplored in the literature for this application, has shown to improve electrochemical (EC) behavior, in some cases doubling the number of cycles in which the anode exhibits reversible EC behavior. Conversely, higher current densities lead to rapid volume changes, and the resulting stress cannot be relieved, thereby affecting the anode's service life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic anode
Diffusion bonded
Aluminum
Copper

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Diffusion bond characterization of bimetallic anodes
Determination of the bimetallic anode specific capacity
Determination of the threshold electrochemical properties of bimetallic anodes

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM-BSE micrographs of the bimetallic anode diffusion affected zone of bimetallic anodes: a) AA1001-Cu, b) AA3005-Cu and c) AA3104-Cu.
GCD curves of the different bimetallic anodes subjected to a full lithiation process at 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2.

Discharge specific capacity and coulombic efficiency of bimetallic anodes controlling the lithiation step to reach 5 % of the experimental capacity at a) 0.5 and b) 1 mA/cm2, repectively. c) GCD curves of the same bimetallic anodes subjected to 0.5 mA/cm2 at the 1st, 50th and 100th cycle.
SEM micrographs of the bimetallic anode exposed surfaces subjected to 100 GCD cycles at 1 mA/cm2 controlling the lithiation step to reach 5 % of the experimental capacity.

Discharge specific capacity and coulombic efficiency of bimetallic anodes controlling the lithiation step to reach 10 % of the experimental capacity at a) 0.5 and b) 1 mA/cm2, repectively. c) GCD curves of the same bimetallic anodes subjected to 0.5 mA/cm2 at the 1st, 50th and 100th cycle.
SEM micrographs of the bimetallic anode exposed surfaces subjected to 100 GCD cycles at 0.5 mAh/cm2 controlling the lithiation step to reach 10 % of the experimental capacity.
Discharge specific capacity and coulombic efficiency of bimetallic anodes controlling the lithiation step to reach 20 % of the experimental capacity at a) 0.5 and b) 1 mA/cm2, repectively.
GCD curves of bimetallic anodes controlling the lithiation step to reach 20 % of the experimental capacity.
SEM micrographs of the bimetallic anode exposed surfaces subjected to 100 GCD cycles at 0.5 mA/cm2 controlling the lithiation step to reach 20 % of the experimental capacity.

Discharge specific capacity and coulombic efficiency of bimetallic anodes controlling the lithiation step to reach 50 % of the experimental capacity at a) 0.5 and b) 1 mA/cm2, repectively. c) GCD curves of the same bimetallic anodes subjected to 1 mA/cm2 at the 1st, 50th and 100th cycle.
SEM micrographs of the bimetallic anode exposed surfaces subjected to 100 GCD cycles at 0.5 mA/cm2 controlling the lithiation step to reach 50 % of the experimental capacity.
Properties of the different alloys used in the study.
Chemical composition of the aluminum studied alloys obtained by XRF technique.
Diffussion bonding conditions and their correspondence with the thickness of the diffusion layers formed for each bimetallic anode.
Relevant electrochemical information obtained from the first and third cycle (1st and 3rd, respectively) of the GCD tests for each bimetallic anode.

head.ce:title: Unveiling the electrochemical threshold properties of novel aluminum‑copper bimetallic foils anodes for high reversibility li-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group on Nano and Multifunctional Composites for Advanced Technologies (AdvNanoComp), Escuela Superior de Ciencias Experimentales y Tecnología, Rey Juan Carlos University, C/Tulipán s/n, 28933, Móstoles, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group on Nano and Multifunctional Composites for Advanced Technologies (AdvNanoComp)
Escuela Superior de Ciencias Experimentales y Tecnología

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science and Engineering Area, ESCET, Rey Juan Carlos University, C/Tulipán s/n, 28933 Móstoles, Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Investigación de Tecnologías para la Sostenibilidad, Universidad Rey Juan Carlos, C/Tulipán s/n, 28933 Móstoles, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto de Investigación de Tecnologías para la Sostenibilidad, Universidad Rey Juan Carlos

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto de Investigación de Tecnologías para la Sostenibilidad, Universidad Rey Juan Carlos, C/Tulipán s/n, 28933 Móstoles, Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: EQUINOR ASA, Research Centre, Arkitekt Ebbellsvei 10, 7053 Trondheim, Norway
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: EQUINOR ASA, Research Centre, Arkitekt Ebbellsvei 10, 7053 Trondheim, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-reversibility aluminum‑copper bimetallic anodes have been characterized to determine the threshold specific capacity necessary to ensure a highly reversible process when used as anodes in Li-ion batteries.

By controlling the intercalation step, the formation of a β-AlLi surface layer on the aluminum side is promoted. This layer effectively mitigates the stress generated during the expansion and contraction associated to the charging/discharging process.

The threshold specific capacity was assessed by applying four different charging steps, corresponding to 5 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 50 % of the experimental specific capacity of aluminum, over 100 galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles. Additionally, the effects of different current densities (0.5 and 1 mA/cm2) and two aluminum qualities (1XXX and 3XXX) on the reversibility of the reactions and capacity loss were analyzed.

The results indicated that charging steps corresponding to a specific capacity of 200 mA.h.g−1 were identified as the threshold capacity for achieving reversible anode behavior. Although this property is the most critical factor, the threshold point also depends on various factors such as the aluminum alloy used for the bimetallic anodes and the current density applied. The use of the 3XXX alloy, which is underexplored in the literature for this application, has shown to improve electrochemical (EC) behavior, in some cases doubling the number of cycles in which the anode exhibits reversible EC behavior. Conversely, higher current densities lead to rapid volume changes, and the resulting stress cannot be relieved, thereby affecting the anode's service life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminum as anode active material have shown promising EC properties for its application in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Aluminum is able to intercalate Li ion in its crystalline structure producing intercalation/deintercalation reversible reaction at low equilibrium potentials (0.23–0.38 V vs. Li/Li+). This process gives rise to the formation for different aluminum‑lithium phases and compounds reaching a high theoretical capacity of at least 993 m.A.h/g [1–3]. Therefore, from its earlies application as anode substantial efforts were made towards the exploration this electrode materials conducting to improve electrodes energy density. Furthermore, its high abundance, low cost, and excellent electrical and thermal conductivities significantly enhance the competitiveness of this metal compared to other anode materials under investigation [4,5].

Despite the potential of aluminum as a lithium storage material, there are few studies that showcase stable high-capacity performance during anode cycling tests. As a result, alternative materials are being explored. However, the intrinsic lithium storage capabilities of aluminum have not been fully leveraged. Despite ongoing research into new anode materials for LIBs, aluminum is often overlooked as a viable option, which is surprising given its remarkable properties [6,7].

Experimental results indicate that pure aluminum anodes often suffer from capacity fading after initial cycles. The causes of this issue have been extensively studied and include: (i) structural damage due to significant volume changes during the charge-discharge cycle, leading to (ii) crack formation and eventual pulverization of the anode, resulting in loss of electrical contact, and (iii) lithium loss due to diffusion into the bulk or formation of stable AlLi phases [8–11]. Various strategies have been proposed to address these challenges, primarily focusing on reducing the size of active anode elements, such as using micrometric or submicrometric aluminum particles and applying very thin films [12–15]. However, the high production cost of these electrodes has significantly hindered their commercial viability.

Incorporating non-active elements into the alloy has also been proposed to mitigate capacity fading. Although this approach may reduce the overall capacity of the anode, it helps manage the volume expansion of the alloy. Adding copper to an aluminum matrix has been reported to have beneficial effects, as copper can participate in lithiation reactions, forming useful ternary active phases. Additionally, the presence of lithium in the active alloy, such as in AlLi alloys, has been noted to inhibit the excessive growth of highly lithiated phases, which are suspected to contribute to capacity fading [16–18].

Following this approach, the use of the 3XXX series as an anode for lithium-ion batteries has been minimally explored. Preliminary electrochemical (EC) studies indicate a high potential for implementation as a reversible anode due to its significant reversibility in intercalation/deintercalation reactions [19,20]. This electrochemical behavior can be attributed to the presence of alloying elements such as Mn and Cu, which effectively mitigate overall alloy volume expansion and promote the formation of highly reversible ternary and quaternary active phases. However, this assertion has only been validated in low cyclability tests, and its applicability to higher galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests remains to be addressed.

Metallic foil anodes has long attracted researchers' attention in lithium rechargeable batteries. However, the commercialization of graphite anodes in lithium-ion batteries (LIB) led the research community to focus on the powder slurry-coating technique.

Recently, foil-type anodes has renewed researcher interest due to their prospects for sustainability, lower manufacturing costs, and high specific capacities compared to nanostructured composite electrodes [6,10,21]. Metal foils are promising anode candidates for replacing graphite in lithium-ion batteries, as metal alloys offer high lithium storage capacity. In addition, their direct use as foils could avoid the need for slurry coating during manufacturing. An additional advantage of aluminum over other alloy anode materials, such as silicon, is the ease ability to manufacture aluminum-based foils [7,22].

In this scenery, the research community has focus on the development of high reversible Al foils by following different approximations. Some of the above-mentioned solutions, such as the of different alloying elements, has also been considered but, others such as the application of annealing treatment achieves a more uniformly distributions of the mechanical stress generated during de-lithiation [14,23]. Then, the capacity loss can be limited to less than 12 % after 200 cycles for a full cell paired with a LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode [9]. Similarly, Li et al. claim that a mechanically hard Al foil can minimize the mechanical damage during the phase transition, thus giving 120 stable cycles when paired with a LiCoO2 (LCO) cathode [6].

In this study, a novel anode structure has been developed by a partial lithiation of the metallic Al foil to form surface β-AlLi reaction layer. The formation of this phase has been studied by different authors and have found its formation on the surface of the Al foil gave rise to a characteristic porous microstructure which help to accommodate the stress generated during the volume variation of the GCD cycles [22–25]. Pre-lithiation can offset irreversible lithium consumption. Additionally, during discharging, Li ions are preferentially extracted from the surface Li-rich phase in the pre-lithiated areas.
However, a full pre-liathiation is usually applied and, progressively, the intercalation/deintercalation process of the Li ion in the Al bulk material gave rise to the pulverization of the anode.

By applying a partial pre-lithiation and limiting the lithiation step to only lithiate the prelithiate area, the generated stress is mainly confined to the porous reaction layer, thereby limiting the stress generated in the bulk aluminum foil and improving the reversibility behavior. Additionally, this approach enables the electrolyte-exposed surface to work as an active layer for lithium storage, which present an adequate morphology characterized by a prous layer, as previously exposed, while the rest of the Al the foil preserve the mechanical intedrity.

This work uses aluminiun copper bimetallic anodes joined by diffusion bonding tecnique as negative electrode. By using this approximation we achive several objetives such as: i) the aluminun enrichment with copper that serve as non active material improving the EC properties, ii) the development of bi-metallic electrode layer structure that could increase the stability of the aluminum phase and iii) copper side of the bimetallic anode can work simuntanuosly as current colletor hence providing an alternative anode materials for lithium batteries.

In this study, we determine the reversibility of copper‑aluminum bimetallic anodes. These anodes aim to enhance electrochemical (EC) behavior by introducing non-active elements, such as manganese from the alloy and copper from the diffusion bond, and by generating the β-AlLi layer on the surface of the aluminum foil. Furthermore, the article assesses the threshold specific capacity of aluminum‑copper bimetallic foils to ensure a highly reversible process as anodes in lithium-ion batteries.

The base materials used for the fabrication of the bimetallic anodes by diffusion bonding technique were aluminum and copper. Three different aluminum quality were considered for manufacturing the anode, two aluminum alloys from the 3XXX quality (AA3005 and AA3104, provided by Aludium and RS components, respectively) and one aluminum alloy from the 1XXX quality (AA1001, provided by RS components) used as reference aluminum material. The selection of the 3XXX quality is based on previous promising results, where high intercalation/deintercalation reversibility was observed when AlCu bimetallic using aluminum 3XXX quality anodes were subjected to CV tests [19]. Both qualities were selected to investigate the effect of various alloying elements on the electrochemical properties of the anodes. In the case of copper, pure commercial quality was selected in this case provided by RS components.
Copper and aluminum sheets, each measuring 50 × 50 mm2, were utilized in this study for the diffusion bonding tests. Table 1 summarizes the relevant material properties for the bonding study.

Diffusion bonding of aluminum and copper sheets were conducted using a Sintris 10STV uniaxial hot press. Prior to the heating cycle, chamber atmosphere was purged three times: vacuum for 10 min followed by filling of with 99,9 % argon purity for 15 min.

The heating ramp for the bonding test was performed as follow:
•
From 20 to 360 °C: 7 °C/ s
•
From 360 to 450 °C: 1 °C/ s
•
From 450 up to bonding temperature: 0.5 °C/ s

To control the stresses generated during the cooling stage, which are associated to the mismatch in the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) of both base alloys, the cooling stage ramp was controlled from the bonding temperature down to 350 °C at a cooling rate of 10 °C/min. Subsequently, the system was allowed to cool down to room temperature (RT) under free cooling conditions.

Sample surface treatments consisted in polishing up to 1 μm with diamond paste followed by chemical cleaning. This last stage was applied to activate both bonding surfaces and remove surface impurities or the typical alumina layer that that naturally forms on aluminum surface. Copper was immersed in 10 vol% H2SO4 and aluminum in a 10 vol% NaOH solution. Additionally, aluminum specimens were deactivated in a 10 vol% HNO3 solution. The objective was to improve the metallurgical interactions at the bonding interface between the two base materials.

The bonding conditions used for the fabrication of the bimetallic anodes for each specific alloy consisted of a bonding temperature of 500 °C and a pressure of 16 MPa, applied in all cases. The only variable parameter was the dwell time. For the AA300-Cu and AA3104-Cu joints, these conditions were applied for 5 min, whereas the AA1060-Cu joints required a dwell time of 30 min to achieve high joint quality.

Those conditions were selected according to previous studies to promote the diffusion mechanisms and control the growth of intermetallic layer formed at the bonding interface when Cu and Al sheets were bonding by diffusion bonding technique [19]. However, bonding parameters needed to be optimized, in this case, due to the different quality and thickness of the aluminum foils used in this study. The conditions ensure high interfacial strength of the joint avoiding delamination or loss of metallic/electrical contact while conserving the highest possible active volume on the aluminiun side.

The electrochemical performance of the bimetallic anodess was assesed using 2025 coins with half-cell configuration. The coins were assembled inside an Ar-filled glovebox to avoid the presence of O2 and H2O. The counter electrodes were made of lithium and glass microfiber filters of 150 mm diameter (supplied by Whatman™) were used as separators. The selected electrolyte was LiPF6 1 M in ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (1:1 v/v). Aluminiun-cooper bimetallic anodes measuring 1 × 1 cm2 were used for the tests.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were performed with the NEWARE BTS4000-5V10MA battery testing system.These tests aim to evaluate the sepecific capacity of the electrodes at both 0.5, 1 mA/cm2 current desnsities aplying 3 complete cycles. The purpose is to determinime both specific capacity and step times for all bimetalic electrodes. Then the fading capacity when applying 100 GCD cycles controlling the duration of the lithiantion step was assesed. The purspose is to determine the treshold litiation range to enhace the formation of the β-AlLi reaction layer at the aluminiun surface that improve the EC properties avoiding the degradation and pulverization of the anode. This procedure enables the use of aluminiun‑copper bimetallic anodes for high reversivility Li-ion batteries. The litiation step was control to reach a certain degree of specific capacity (5 %, 10 %, 20 % and 50 %) with respect to the maximun specific capacity determined for each specific alloy and current density. To evaluate the cycle life of the bimetallic anodes and determine their electrochemical threshold properties, a specific capacity retention criterion was established. An anode was considered acceptable if its capacity attenuation remained below 60 % of its initial capacity in each experiment. All electrochemical tests were conducted at 25 °C temperature.

After EC tests the anodes integrity, the presence of reaction layer or third phases in the anodes surfaces were evaluated by SEM technique using a Scanning Electron Microscopy at 15 keV (SEM, S3400 Hitachi) equipped with an Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX, Quantax) analyser.

For quality assurance and to facilitate a better discussion of the results, experimental compositions of the aluminum base materials were determined using X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF, Philips Magix Pro).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diffusion bond characterization of bimetallic anodes
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the bonding and electrochemical characterization of the bimetallic anodes, XRF analysis was conducted to the aluminiun and copper parents foils to experimentally determine the chemical composition of the alloys, ensuring accuracy in the discussion and interpretation of the results. Table 2 resumes the chemical composition results of the different alloys. This result aligns with the reference composition provided by ASTM [26].

Fig. 1 a, b and c show SEM images of the cross-section diffusion-bonded specimens for all the bimetallic anodes characterized in this work AA1001-Cu, AA3005-Cu and AA3104-Cu, respectively. The micrographs, focus on the diffusion affected zone of each anode using the bonding conditions described in the experimental section, show the presence of different phases formed at the diffusion bonded interface between aluminum and copper. The analysis of the microstructure using the back scattering detector (BSE) shown the presence of copper, being the element with the highest atomic number in the system, appearing with a bright contrast, while aluminum appears darker. In this last parent material, the presence of homogeneously distributed micro precipitates is clearly observed. The typical low solubility of alloying elements in aluminum and the slow cooling ramps used during the bonding tests gave rise to the precipitation of the alloying presumably in its thermodynamic stable form, typically Al8Mn5 and Al12Mn precipitates in the case of the 3XXX alloy, which shows the highest presence of secondary phases in accordance with the higher alloying content of the alloy [27]. This process leads to a rise in the nucleation and coarsening of precipitates from solvus temperature down to room temperature, reaching the microscale.

A high degree of continuity between both parent alloys is observed in all specimens indicating the consecution of sufficient interfacial strength at the bonded area. All bonded specimens exhibit similar microstructures with continuous diffusion layer formation. Although AA3005-Cu anode shows the formation of 2 main diffusion layers, (1 and 3 in Fig. 1b), AA1001-Cu and AA3104-Cu anodes show the formation of an additional one (2 in Fig. 1a and c) nucleated and grown between the two main diffusion layers previously identified. Considering the semi-quantitative EDS microanalysis (at. %: 66Al34Cu, 46Al52Cu, and 35Al65Cu and for phase numbers 3, 2, and 1, respectively) and the solubility limits and crystallo-graphic phases identified by Zobac et al. [28] in the AlCu binary system, these phases can be identified as θ (Al side), η2 and γ1 (Cu side) for phases number 3, 2 and 1, respectively.

Table 3 presents the measurements of the total thicknesses determined from the SEM images for each bonding alloy. The results indicate the control of the diffusion layers growth, which is limited to 7–11 μm range. Therefore, these bonding conditions ensures the conservation of a high active mass proportion on the aluminum side while reducing the brittleness of the joint, which is crucial for foils anode manipulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the bimetallic anode specific capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the GCD curves of the different bimetallic anodes subjected to a full lithiation process at 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2. The main purpose of this experiment is to determine the specific capacity of each alloy at the studied current densities and the times involve for each step.

All samples show the presence of the two main plateaus associated to the charge and discharge process in each case. During charging, the potential increases rapidly up to 0.5 V, where the deintercalation of Li takes place giving rise to the corresponding plateau. During the reverse step, the plateau corresponding to the intercalation reaction occurred at 0.1 V. Both processes occurred in accordance with the data found in the literature [10]. No sign of secondary reaction associated to the presence of the different alloying elements has been observed.

Although only 3 GCD cycles has been applied some differences in the EC behavior is observed. The AA1001-Cu anode gave rise to a rapid fading capacity, especially during the third cycle of the tests. The charging capacity decrease from the initial 720 and 790 mAh/g to approximately 600 mAh/g in both cases for the 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2 current density, respectively.

The mechanisms responsible of the alumnium rapid fading capacity process has been widely studied and include different process such as pulverization or formation of AlLi stable phases. Although both mechasisms contributed to the loss of specific capacity, the first one mechanism is the responsible of the rapid capacity loss associated to the deterioration the anode integrity. During Al/LiAl (α/β) phase transformations associate to the lithiation and delithiation process aluminum experimented a considerable volume variation. This volume modification could reach high rates up to 96 % in the case of high capacity anodes, which is the case of the aluminum material [8,10,29]. This volume modification induces the accumulation of internal stress. In principle, bulk base material could accommodate the stress by accumulation of the internal stress or internal plastic deformation. However, the hardening effect associated to the plastic deformation, could induce the formation of cracks which, at some point, could lead to the pulverization of the bulk material and the loss of electrical conductivity.

In addition, the formation of new surfaces as a consequence of crack formation and material pulverization gave rise to the formation of a secondary solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth leading to poor coulombic efficiency and fast capacity fade [21].

The anaylsis of the 3XXX serie reported a different EC behavior in this case. Both alloys exhibited an initial loss in discharge capacity, particularly the AA3104-Cu anode. This behavior is attributed to the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) and stable AlLi phases. However, after this initial drop, the capacity appeared to stabilize over the following two galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) cycles.

The analysis of the coulombic efficiency (CE), shown in Table 4 for the third cycle of each experiment, supports this observation. CE values of approximately 99 % were recorded for the 3XXX series alloy in all cases. In contrast, the 1XXX series alloy showed CE values in the range of 94–96 %, indicating a loss in reaction reversibility from the early stages of testing.

As it was previously exposed, this EC behavior can be attributed to the presence of the alloying elements, such as Cu and Mn, that effectively reduce the overall alloy volume expansion and promotes the formation of highly reversible ternary/quaternary active phases. In this specific case, the AA3005 alloy exhibited improved electrochemical performance, with virtually no capacity loss observed over the three GCD cycles. This behavior may be attributed to the higher overall alloying content of AA3005, with the increased presence of Mn being the most significant factor potentially contributing to the enhanced electrochemical stability. Additionally, the higher Fe content could also play a role, as Fe does not directly participate in the electrochemical reactions, potentially mitigating overall volume expansion, as previously discussed. In order to promote the reversible behavior of aluminum as active material anode in LIBs, several studies have pointed out that the formation of porous β-LiAl reaction layer on the aluminum exposed surface could help to accommodate the stress generated during the volume expansion [30]. This layer, formed by a prelithiation of the bulk material, growth from the anode exposed surface covering only a fraction of the aluminum foil. Depending on the lithiation step duration, further lithiation cycles will confine the intercalation of the Li ions in this layer or will penetrate into the bulk material, initiating the degradation mechanism. By controlling the lithiation step the formation and growth of this layer could be controlled in order to enhance the reversibility of the reaction while conserving, as much as possible, the specific capacity of the anode.

Table 4 shows the data obtained during the first cycle of the GCD tests including the specific capacity and the duration of each step. The duration of the lithiation step of the first cycle have been used to calculate the duration of the different steps to reach different ratios of specific capacity with respect to the experimental one calculated. Then, lithiation steps corresponding to specific capacities of 5, 10, 20 and 50 % of the total experimental capacity will be used for the following GCD tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the threshold electrochemical properties of bimetallic anodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the discharge capacity obtained from the GCD tests applied for 100 cycles to the bimetallic anodes. In this case the tests were conducted controlling the lithiation step to reach 5 % of the experimental capacity by using 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2 current densities (Fig. 3a and b, respectively). By restricting the intercalation step, the lithiation process is confined to the outermost layer of the aluminum foil surface. This approach facilitates the formation of a β-LiAl porous reaction layer in this region, which exhibits high reversibility in intercalation and deintercalation reactions. Consequently, this method helps to mitigate or reduce the stress within the bulk material, thereby preventing the primary mechanism responsible for the rapid capacity fading observed in aluminum.

The results indicated that the specific capacity remained constant over 100 cycles at both 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2 current densities. There were no significant differences between the various bimetallic anodes used in this study under these GCD test conditions. These findings contrast with the previously mentioned scenario (full lithiation of the foil), where a clear loss in specific capacity was observed after just 2 or 3 cycles for the AA1001-Cu anode. In these cases, no signs of capacity fading were observed, demonstrating excellent electrochemical behavior when using aluminum as the active anode material.

Under these conditions, the bimetallic anodes exhibited an aerial capacity of approximately 1 mAh/cm2, placing them within the same range as other studies in the literature that have demonstrated highly reversible reactions of aluminum foils for at least 100 cycles. These studies also applied similar current densities for this purpose [6,30]. The coulombic efficiency remained constant, with values close to 98 % throughout the entire test.

Fig. 3c presents examples of the GCD curves of samples subjected to 0.5 mA/cm2 at the 1st, 50th, and 100th cycles, those results were also representatives for 1 mA/cm2 current density samples. Initially, all samples exhibited similar behavior, with no signs of secondary reactions or capacity fading. The initial cycles reported lower capacities, which could be attributed to the loss of some lithium ions in the bulk material that could not be extracted in subsequent cycles. However, in all cases, the 50th and 100th cycles displayed similar profiles, indicating that the charge and discharge steps followed the same reaction mechanism, thereby preserving the integrity of the anode.

After the tests, the anodes were extracted from the coin cell, and the integrity and surface interactions of the aluminum foil were analyzed using both a stereoscopic microscope and SEM. The results shown in Fig. 4 for the samples subjected to 1 mA/cm2 indicated that the anode's integrity was preserved after the tests. The characteristic metallic surface of aluminum turned black due to the formation of β-LiAl on the aluminum surface. High-magnification SEM images, obtained from the red dashed square in each case, show the formation of a porous layer. These layers did not exhibit crack formation or the initiation of the pulverization process after 100 cycles. The characteristic morphology of this layer, displaying a porous structure, is responsible for the high reversibility achieved. Some SEM images, such as those of the AA1001-Cu and AA3104-Cu samples, also showed the presence of glass fibers that had detached from the separator during the extraction of the anode from the coin cell.
•
10 % of the experimental capacity

The increase of the lithiation step to reach 10 % of the anodes experimental capacity gave rise to similar EC response to that obtained in the previous case (Fig. 5 a and b for 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2, respectively). The specific and aerial capacity reached under those conditions were approximately 110 mAh/g and 2 mAh/cm2, respectively. This represents double the capacity of the results reported in the literature, which achieved high reversible reactions and maintaining the capacity higher than 90 % for at least 100 cycles using aluminum foils [6,30].

It is noticeable that AA1001-Cu anode showed lower coulombic efficiency than the other two samples reporting values of approximately 95 % in some steps of the tests. However, this lower efficiency is not accompanied by loss of capacity. Explanation could be associated to the loss of Li ion in the bulk material, formation of stable phases but more likely to the deterioration of the anode integrity. The formation of new anode surface, as a consequence of crack formation, supposes the generation of new SEI that consume Li from the electrolyte, reducing the coulombic efficiency. That means that in the case of the AA1001-Cu anode, although the EC results have not been compromised, the crack formation mechanism has started, and nucleation of crack have occurred producing new electrode-electrolyte surface consuming Li ions. This process is expected to be in the earliest stage where cracks do not produce pulverization or loss of electrical contact.

The analysis of the GCD curves for samples tested at a current density of 0.5 mA/cm2, shown in Fig. 5c, reveals a similar voltage profile across all cases. This behavior is also representative of the samples tested at 1 mA/cm2, indicating consistent electrochemical performance across different current densities. Once again, there are no signs of secondary reactions or anode degradation, and the lower charge capacity associated with the 1st cycle is again observed. Only the AA1001-Cu anode exhibited a slight loss of charge capacity in the 100th cycle compared to the 50th cycle. Although this represents only a minor capacity loss, it could be linked to the previously described mechanism and the initiation of the pulverization process, which might compromise the anode's integrity. The remaining anodes displayed similar charge and discharge profiles in both the 50th and 100th cycles. There are not significant differences between the application of both studied current densities. In principle, the application of higher current densities gives rise to volume expansion and shrinkage process in a shorter period of time inducing the generation of residual stress more difficult to accommodate. It seems that the generation of the porous reaction layer helps to mitigate this phenomenon, at least by controlling the lithiation step to reach 5 and 10 % of the experimental capacity. This mitigation seems to be enough to not experiment differences between both conditions.

The characterization of the anodes integrity after the GCD tests is shown in Fig. 6 . In this case, clear evidences of the crack nucleation and propagation is observed in all samples. The stereoscopic microscope images already reveal differences. While the AA1001-Cu sample shows only a small area covered by aluminum active material in the center of the anode, both anodes from the 3XXX series show that the active material still covers the entire surface. In the case of the anode from the 1XXX series, the copper substrate is visible in some areas, where the aluminum has been detached from the anode. It should be noted that, according to the literature and the experimental observations of this study, the growth of the AlLi layer from the native aluminum surface occurs by penetrating both into the bulk material and into the electrolyte, resulting in the characteristic porous microstructure.

As the anode is in contact with the glass fiber separator, some glass fibers could be incorporated in the layer and detached from the separator during the anode extraction. Those fiber could be observed in both stereoscopic and SEM imagens of the anodes after the GCD tests. The presence of cracks in the anodes, although initially not affecting the electrochemical behavior due to the absence of the pulverization step, can compromise the integrity of the anode. This could explain why, during the extraction of the anode from the coin cell, part of the aluminum layer detached, leaving only a fraction of the aluminum visible in the AA1001-Cu anode. This observation aligns with the lower coulombic efficiency noted during the test, which could be attributed to the creation of new anode surfaces as a result of crack formation, thereby reducing the coulombic efficiency at those moments.

In the case to both 3XXX anode quality, the higher CE indicated lower crack formation and therefore higher mechanical integrity of the anodes is expected, which agree with the fact that the extraction of the anode has been achieved without detaching aluminum active material.

SEM images of the exposed surface indicated the presence of a crack network in all samples. For the anodes of the 3XXX series, the cracks appear to penetrate only a few microns within the focus range of the equipment. Therefore, although the presence of cracks is clearly observed, they do not affect the electrochemical behavior or the overall integrity of the anode. In contrast, the observed cracks in the AA1001-Cu anode, although narrower, are expected to penetrate deeper into the aluminum foil, affecting both the electrochemical and mechanical integrity. In addition, the observation could correspond to internal observations of the aluminum active material, as most of the active material has detached.
•
20 % of the experimental capacity

The increase of the lithiation step to reach 20 % of the experimental capacity of aluminum result on a completely different EC behavior. The results shown in Fig. 7 a and b for 0.5 and 1 mA/cm2, respectively indicated that the threshold condition for an EC reversible behavior of aluminum have been reached. The applied conditions corresponded to a specific and aerial capacity of approximately 225 mAh/g and 4 mAh/cm2, which is superior to the data found in the bibliography [6,30].

Although all samples exhibit a capacity loss process, significant differences between the anodes are observed. The bimetallic anode fabricated with AA1001 aluminum foils demonstrated a capacity fading process at lower number of cycles for both studied current densities. In particular, for this anode, a severe capacity fading process is observed after 50 cycles when using 0.5 mA/cm2. In contrast, bimetallic anodes fabricated with 3XXX alloy quality reported similar EC behavior maintaining reversible behavior up to 85 cycles.

The application of the highest current density led to a more rapid deterioration of the electrochemical performance in the AA1001-Cu anode. In this case, a severe capacity fading process was observed from the beginning of the test, with the anode failing almost immediately and showing negligible capacity retention after 50 cycles.

Conversely, the bimetallic anodes fabricated with 3XXX series aluminum alloys demonstrated improved electrochemical behavior. However, differences between the two 3XXX-based anodes were evident. The AA3104-Cu anode exhibited capacity fading after approximately 60 cycles, followed by a progressive decline to only a few mAh/g by the end of the test (100 cycles). In contrast, the AA3005-Cu anode, despite showing more scattered capacity retention after 85 cycles, appeared to maintain its capacity until the end of the test.

The analysis of the coulombic efficiency reported values lower than 90 % from the beginning of the tests in the case of the AA1001-Cu anode at both current densities. This is an indication that the integrity of the anode was compromised from the beginning, which could be associated to the stress generated during the volume modifications. For the anodes made from 3XXX series alloys, a scattered behavior was observed after approximately 50 cycles in both instances. This behavior corresponds to the onset of the capacity loss process in all cases. Consequently, both phenomena can be attributed to the formation of a crack network, which creates new exposed surfaces. Eventually, the pulverization of aluminum disrupts the electrical contact, leading to a reduction in capacity.

The reported improvement in the electrochemical behavior of anodes fabricated with 3XXX alloys is attributed to the effect of the Mn alloying element and the diffusion bonding technique used to join aluminum to copper. It has been documented that the incorporation of non-active phases in aluminum anodes plays a beneficial role in preventing anode deterioration [9,10,13]. These phases tend to form reversible ternary or quaternary phases and, as non-active phases, they reduce overall volume changes, thereby mitigating the stress generated. Consequently, these anodes exhibit improved reversible behavior and overall integrity, mitigating their deterioration through the aforementioned mechanism and thereby extending the component's lifespan.

The GCD curves corresponding to all anodes tested is shown in Fig. 8 . In this case different EC behaviors were observed depending on the applied current density, therefore its analysis should be performed separately for each condition. The application of the lower current density reported similar tendency in all cases, where the curve corresponding to 1st and 50th cycle indicated the same tendency than in previous conditions. However, the deterioration of the anodes after this point made that the 100th curve showed considerably lower specific capacity, in special the curve of the AA1001-Cu anode did not show the characteristic plateau at 0.6 V reporting a negligible specific capacity. The anodes corresponding to the 3XXX aluminum quality reported capacity in the 30–50 mAh/g range at this point.

The increase of the current density gave rise to different behavior. AA1001-Cu and AA3104-Cu anodes showed a faster deterioration of the EC properties and, consequently, loss of the specific capacity. This fact is evidence by studying the curve for the 50th and 100th cycle. In the case of the AA1001-Cu both curves did not show almost any capacity retention after 100 cycles and, in the case of the AA3104-Cu anode, only 25 mAh/g is retained. On the other hand, AA3005-Cu anode showed similar curve profile than that obtained for the 50th cycle, therefore the scatter behavior shown in the Fig. 8 should be associate to the formation of new surfaces that did not produce pulverization of the anode causing the loss of the electrical conductivity.

The examination of the anodes integrity after the GCD tests is shown in Fig. 9 . The overall results indicated that the integrity of the anodes has been considerably affected by the tests, in agreement with the EC behavior previously discussed.

In the case of the AA1001-Cu and AA3005-Cu anodes, copper was easily identified in certain areas, indicating complete detachment of the aluminum material in those zones. The rest of the surface exhibited a thin aluminum layer, likely the remaining aluminum that adhered after anode extraction. These zones, including the AA-3104 anode, displayed the characteristic porosity of the β-AlLi reaction layer, indicating that after the degradation of the most active volume, the intercalation/deintercalation process continued with the remaining aluminum material. In these instances, the formation of the reaction layer occurred at the new electrode-electrolyte interface, which was responsible for the remaining specific capacity reported at the end of the test, particularly in the AA3104-Cu and AA3005-Cu cases. These findings align with the progressive degradation and creation of new electrode/electrolyte interfaces, which resulted in low coulombic efficiency reported during the GCD tests.
•
50 % of the experimental capacity

The application of the longest lithiation step up to reach 50 % of the experimental capacity of aluminum gave rise to a rapid deterioration of the EC properties of the anodes. Fig. 10 shows the specific capacity evolution of the anodes at both studied current densities a) 0.5 mA.h/cm2 and b) 1 mA.h/cm2 for the 100 cycles of the tests. The applied lithiation step gave rise, in this case, to specific and aerial capacities of 450–500 mAh/g and 10 mAh/cm2, respectively.

The findings demonstrated a rapid deterioration of the anode capacity due to the investigated degradation mechanism. The deeper penetration of Li ions into the anodes resulted in the generation of higher stress from volume changes process. This stress predominantly occurs within the bulk material rather than the anode surface, unlike previous instances. This is attributed to the deeper Li ion penetration and the fact that the reaction layer forming only on the outermost surface of the aluminum foils. Consequently, the formation of the reaction layer, which could mitigate this phenomenon, only partially alleviates the generated stress in this case.

In all cases a fading capacity phenomenon is observed from the beginning of the tests. However, several differences can be identified depending on the anode. In the case of applying 0.5 mA.h/cm2 to AA1001-Cu anode, the results reported an abrupt loss of the capacity from the beginning of the test. After 10 cycles the capacity retention is approximately 150 mAh/g and 50 mAh/g after 25 cycles. The remaining capacity progressively decreases up to almost 0 mAh/g after 50 cycles.

The electrochemical response of anodes fabricated from the 3XXX aluminum series exhibited a similar trend in both instances. The initial capacity slightly decreased up to 25 cycles, after which there was a sharp decline, reaching nearly 0 mAh/g after 50 cycles.
In all cases the coulombic efficiency showed a scatter behavior reporting values from 80 to 100 % associated to the formation of new solid-electrolyte interfaces from the beginning of the tests.

The application of a current density of 1 mA/cm2 demonstrated similar electrochemical behavior, where all anodes exhibited a progressive loss of capacity during the first 25 cycles. Beyond this point, the capacity loss became more abrupt, reaching nearly 0 mAh/g after 50 cycles.

The analysis of the GCD curves indicated that only for the 1st cycle of each anode the characteristic charge-discharge curve is obtained (Fig. 10c). The information obtained for the 50th and 100th cycle indicated that the degradation of the anodes has occurred and no evidence of charging and discharging process is observed.

The observation of the anodes by stereoscopic microscopy after the tests revealed that almost all aluminum has been detached from the anode because of the degradation and pulverization of the active material (Fig. 11 ). Consequently, copper base material is easily identified in almost all anode surfaces. This process has led to the failure of the anode previously discussed in Fig. 10.

The analyses of the remaining aluminum active material by SEM revealed that in the case of AA1001-Cu and AA3005-Cu anode (areas marked as 2 in Fig. 11) the characteristic porous reaction layer is conserved in those areas. It is expected that the crack network observed by using shorter lithiation steps has, in this case, further deteriorated the integrity of the anode up to its complete pulverization and, during the extraction of the anode from the coin cell, only local areas of the anode remained covered by aluminum.

In the case of the AA3104-Cu anode the area covered by this phase is considerably higher than that of the other anodes, however, the analysis of the capacity evolution and GCD curves indicated that the discontinuities in the electrical conductivity paths should be the responsible of the capacity loss in those areas.

Diffusion bonded technique has been used to join aluminum to copper to produce bimetallic anodes for application in Li ion batteries. The selected bonded conditions ensure the consecution of a high metallic continuity while controlling the growth of the intermetallic layer at the bonded interface. Thus, the volume of aluminum active material is conserved avoiding excessive brittleness, necessary for the anode foil manipulation.

By controlling the duration of the lithiation step the formation β-AlLi surface reaction layer, exhibiting a porous morphology, is promoted. The characteristics of this layer enable the reversible behavior after applying different charging/discharging cycles due to effectively mitigate the stress generated during the expansion and reduction of the active material volume avoiding the pulverization mechanism leading to the anode failure.

However, this reversible behavior strongly depends on the bulk lithiation of the foil. By increasing the lithiation step the ions tend to intercalate in the bulk material rather than in the reaction layer, inducing stress by volume variation and leading to the anode failure.

The application of lithiation steps corresponding to 5 % and 10 % of the experimental specific capacity, gave rise to a complete EC reversible behavior of the anodes, and no fading capacity of the anodes were observed up to 100 cycles. The examination of the anodes integrity after the tests revealed that the reaction layer formed were preserved and only some cracks were detected not affecting the EC properties. Those conditions correspond to specific capacities of 50 and 110 mAh/g, respectively.

The increasing of the lithiation steps up to reach 20 % and 50 % of the experimental capacity gave rise to the anode failure before the tests were ended. The overall data indicated that, in the first case after 75 cycles all anodes failed. When the highest lithiation step is applied this fact occurred after 50 cycles. In those cases, the specific capacities showed by the anodes were approximately 200 and 450 mAh/g, respectively.

Although the specific capacity is the most determining factor, this threshold point also depends on different factors such as the aluminum alloy used for the bimetallic anodes and the current density used. The use of the 3XXX alloy, poorly explored by the literature for this application, has proved to improve the reversible EC behavior double, in some cases, the number of cycles in which the anode exhibits a reversible EC behavior. This enhancement is particularly evident in the AA3005 alloy. The higher manganese content, along with other alloying elements, contributes to limiting or mitigating volume expansion, which is a key factor in premature anode failure.
Finally, the use of higher current densities implies fast volume variations, and the stress generated could not be relieved affecting the service life of the anode.

Javier de Prado: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Børre T. Børresen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Victoria Utrilla: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Alejandro Ureña: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
